title: Income Inequality Trends in Germany (Gini Coefficient)
summary_md:
"Germanyĺs most recent Gini coefficient reported in major international datasets is 32.4 (2020). The Gini coefficient ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 100 (perfect inequality), so a value in the low 30s indicates moderate income inequality relative to other advanced economies. While Germany remains less unequal than many OECD countries, income dispersion remains significant, particularly when considering housing costs and regional disparities.
Over the past decade, inequality has risen slightly. Estimates indicate the Gini increased from approximately 30.6 in 2011 to 32.4 in 2020. Structural drivers include labor market segmentation, education and skill disparities, and housing affordability pressures in major cities. Although Germanyĺs tax and transfer system reduces inequality relative to market income, redistribution has not fully offset wage dispersion and demographic changes.
Measurement considerations include differences between market and disposable income, use of equivalized household income, potential underreporting of top incomes, and survey methodology changes over time.

title: Income Inequality in Mexico
summary_md: "Mexicoĺs most recent Gini coefficient is 44.01 (2022) based on net income per capita. A Gini of 44 indicates relatively high-income inequality. Although inequality in Mexico is lower than the very high levels seen in Latin America, it remains high compared to most OECD countries. This indicates that income inequality remains significant and rooted in long-standing structural factors.  Over the past decade, inequality in Mexico has gradually declined. According to the World Bank and OECD, disposable income inequality has gradually declined since the mid-2010s. Recent reductions are associated with significant minimum wage increases beginning in 2019, expansion of social transfer programs, and record levels of remittances. However, structural factors, particularly labor market informality, regional disparities between northern and southern states, and limited fiscal redistribution, continue to limit larger reductions in inequality. Although the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted labor markets, it did not permanently increase inequality, and the broader downward trend resumed afterward.  Measurement considerations are important when interpreting this estimate. The Gini coefficient is based on net income rather than consumption and is calculated on a per-capita basis instead of equivalized household income. Additionally, survey limitations and potential underreporting of high incomes may result in an understatement of true inequality."

title: Income Inequality in Argentina
summary_md: "Argentinaĺs latest available data on its Gini coefficient estimate is 42.4 for 2024, as reported by the World Bank. The scale for inequality ranges from 0 to 100, where 0 represents perfect equality and 100 represents perfect inequality. Therefore, Argentinaĺs Gini coefficient of 42.4 represents a moderately high level of inequality in income distribution, which implies that income is unevenly distributed across households in Argentina (World Bank, 2024).  Over the last ten years, inequality in Argentina has been dynamic due to economic instability, inflation, and issues in the employment sector. There was a slight decline in inequality from 2018 to 2022, during which Argentinaĺs Gini coefficient decreased from 41.7 to 40.7. However, recent economic instability in Argentina, which is characterized by high inflation and currency instability, may have contributed to inequality in Argentina (OECD, 2025; World Bank, 2024). Labor market segmentation, inequality in access to education, and economic inequality between urban and rural areas are some of the structural issues in Argentina that contribute to inequality in Argentina.  While interpreting Argentinaĺs inequality data using the Gini coefficient, it should be taken into consideration that inequality data in Argentina is primarily estimated using household survey data on household income, which may not be accurate in reflecting high-income households in Argentina. Moreover, household survey data in Argentina may be subject to some limitations in that it may only capture data from households in urban areas of Argentina."

title: Income Inequality in New Zealand
summary_md: "The most recent estimate of income inequality in New Zealand is a Gini coefficient of 32.0 for 2020, based on data from the World Income Inequality Database (WIID). The Ginicoefficient ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 100 (perfect inequality), meaning New Zealandĺs value indicates a moderate level of inequality. This places the country close to the middle of OECD economies, with a value slightly above the OECD average of around 31 and similar to Australiaĺs 31.8. This suggests that income distribution in New Zealand is relatively balanced compared with many developed countries, although meaningful disparities still exist across different groups in the population.  Over the past decade, inequality in New Zealand has been relatively stable with moderate fluctuations. Evidence shows inequality rose slightly from the late 2000s into the mid-2010s and then declined, leaving levels in the early 2020s slightly lower than in 2008. Structural factors shaping these trends include labor market differences across industries, educational attainment, and regional economic opportunities, particularly between major cities and rural areas. Government policies such as progressive taxation and transfers like Working for Families and New Zealand Superannuation have helped reduce some income disparities. However, rising housing costs remain an important factor influencing inequality and financial pressure for lower-income households.  Several methodological issues affect how inequality is measured. First, many estimates rely on household income surveys, which can differ in methodology across years and affect comparability. Second, inequality statistics are typically based on income rather than consumption, even though consumption may better capture living standards. Third, results often use equivalized household income, adjusting for household size and composition, which can change inequality estimates depending on the method used."
